---
title: "Solutions — Exercise Sheet 10: Quantum Statistics"
subtitle: "EP4: Experimental Physics 4 — Atomic and Molecular Physics"
---

## Solution 1: Identical Particles and Symmetry

**(a)** Bosons (symmetric): $|\Psi_B\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|\alpha\rangle_1|\beta\rangle_2 + |\beta\rangle_1|\alpha\rangle_2)$

Fermions (antisymmetric): $|\Psi_F\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|\alpha\rangle_1|\beta\rangle_2 - |\beta\rangle_1|\alpha\rangle_2)$

**(b)** If $|\alpha\rangle = |\beta\rangle$: $|\Psi_F\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|\alpha\rangle_1|\alpha\rangle_2 - |\alpha\rangle_1|\alpha\rangle_2) = 0$.

This is the **Pauli exclusion principle**: two identical fermions cannot occupy the same quantum state.

**(c)** Including spin (two spin states per level), the three fermions fill: two in $n = 0$ (spin up and down), one in $n = 1$.

$E = 2 \times \frac{1}{2}\hbar\omega + 1 \times \frac{3}{2}\hbar\omega = \frac{5}{2}\hbar\omega$

## Solution 2: Fermi–Dirac and Bose–Einstein Distributions

**(a)** At $T = 0$: $\bar{n}_{\text{FD}} = 1$ for $\varepsilon < \mu$ and $0$ for $\varepsilon > \mu$ (sharp step). At finite $T$: the step is smeared over a width $\sim k_BT$. At $\varepsilon = \mu$: $\bar{n}_{\text{FD}} = 1/2$.

$\bar{n}_{\text{BE}}$ diverges as $\varepsilon \to \mu$ and has no upper bound.

**(b)** $T_F = E_F/k_B = 7.0/(8.617\times10^{-5}) = 8.12\times10^4\;\text{K}$

$k_BT/E_F = 300/81{,}200 = 0.0037$. Only about $0.4\%$ of the electrons are thermally excited at room temperature. This explains why the electronic contribution to the specific heat of metals is small.

**(c)** When $(\varepsilon-\mu)\gg k_BT$, $e^{(\varepsilon-\mu)/(k_BT)} \gg 1$, so $\pm 1$ in the denominator is negligible:

$\bar{n}_{\text{FD}} \approx \bar{n}_{\text{BE}} \approx e^{-(\varepsilon-\mu)/(k_BT)} = \bar{n}_{\text{MB}}$. ✓

## Solution 3: Bose–Einstein Condensation

**(a)** $k_BT_c = \frac{2\pi\hbar^2}{m}\left(\frac{n}{\zeta(3/2)}\right)^{2/3}$

$= \frac{2\pi(1.055\times10^{-34})^2}{1.44\times10^{-25}}\left(\frac{2.5\times10^{18}}{2.612}\right)^{2/3}$

$\frac{2\pi\hbar^2}{m} = \frac{2\pi\times1.113\times10^{-68}}{1.44\times10^{-25}} = 4.855\times10^{-43}$

$(n/\zeta)^{2/3} = (9.57\times10^{17})^{2/3} = (9.57)^{2/3}\times10^{11.8} = 4.63\times10^{11.8} \approx 2.92\times10^{12}$

$k_BT_c = 4.855\times10^{-43}\times2.92\times10^{12} = 1.42\times10^{-30}\;\text{J}$

$T_c = 1.42\times10^{-30}/(1.381\times10^{-23}) = 1.03\times10^{-7}\;\text{K} = 103\;\text{nK}$

**(b)** $N_0/N = 1 - (T/T_c)^{3/2} = 1 - (1/2)^{3/2} = 1 - 0.354 = 0.646$

About $64.6\%$ of the atoms are in the condensate.

**(c)** At $T = T_c$:

$\lambda_{\text{dB}} = h/\sqrt{2\pi m k_BT_c} = 6.626\times10^{-34}/\sqrt{2\pi\times1.44\times10^{-25}\times1.42\times10^{-30}}$

$= 6.626\times10^{-34}/\sqrt{1.285\times10^{-55}} = 6.626\times10^{-34}/3.585\times10^{-28} = 1.85\times10^{-6}\;\text{m}$

$d = n^{-1/3} = (2.5\times10^{18})^{-1/3} = 7.37\times10^{-7}\;\text{m}$

$\lambda_{\text{dB}}/d = 1.85/0.737 = 2.5$

The de Broglie wavelength exceeds the interparticle spacing — the wave packets overlap, confirming the onset of quantum degeneracy. ✓

## Solution 4: Blackbody Radiation as a Bose Gas

**(a)** Energy density in $[\nu, \nu+d\nu]$:

$u(\nu)d\nu = h\nu \times g(\nu) \times \bar{n}_{\text{BE}}(\nu) \times d\nu = h\nu\times\frac{8\pi\nu^2}{c^3}\times\frac{1}{e^{h\nu/(k_BT)}-1}d\nu$

$$
u(\nu) = \frac{8\pi h\nu^3}{c^3}\frac{1}{e^{h\nu/(k_BT)}-1}
$$

This is the Planck law. ✓

**(b)** $u_{\text{total}} = \int_0^\infty u(\nu)d\nu = \frac{8\pi h}{c^3}\int_0^\infty\frac{\nu^3}{e^{h\nu/(k_BT)}-1}d\nu$

Substituting $x = h\nu/(k_BT)$:

$= \frac{8\pi h}{c^3}\left(\frac{k_BT}{h}\right)^4\int_0^\infty\frac{x^3}{e^x-1}dx = \frac{8\pi^5 k_B^4}{15c^3h^3}T^4 = aT^4$

where $a = 8\pi^5 k_B^4/(15c^3h^3) = 7.566\times10^{-16}\;\text{J/(m}^3\text{K}^4)$.

**(c)** Wien's displacement law: $\nu_{\text{max}} = 2.821\,k_BT/h = 2.821\times1.381\times10^{-23}\times2.725/(6.626\times10^{-34}) = 160\;\text{GHz}$

Wavelength: $\lambda = c/\nu = 1.9\;\text{mm}$. This is in the **microwave** range — hence the name "cosmic microwave background."
